coord_build: GRCh37/hg19 (exact bp position not given in main text; region spans SMARCA2/VLDLR per Figure 5C, chr9:~2.0-2.7Mb per Table S5 VLDLR entry)
gws_threshold: p < 5e-8 (not met; this is one of >5 independent low-frequency (<5%) signals with p<1e-3 flagged as above-95%-CI in the resequencing QQ plot, i.e. suggestive/exploratory)
beta_units: M index scale, targeted resequencing association, additive model
evidence_quote: there are >5 independent low-frequency signals with p < 1e-3 within/near SMARCA2 and VLDLR, with rs7866411 (p = 8.91e-05) and rs2093835 (p = 1.17e-04) being most significantly associated
classification_notes: Paper cites HaploReg-inferred enhancer/DNase peaks in skin (incl. melanocytes/keratinocytes) overlapping tag/LD-linked SNPs (Table S6/S6B), but this is generic epigenomic-annotation overlap, not a demonstrated eQTL/reporter/CRISPR effect on SMARCA2 or VLDLR specifically at this SNP. Separately, paper cites literature-level biological plausibility: SMARCA2 (SWI/SNF component) is recruited by MITF to promoters of TYR/TYRP1/DCT (Vachtenheim et al., 2010 cited); VLDLR knockout mice show hypopigmented retinas (Xia et al., 2013 cited). These are candidate-gene-biology arguments for the GENE, not a demonstrated regulatory link at this SNP. Attempted lookup of rs7866411/rs2093835 in S6B fine-mapping table (region names NOX5/RAB8B/SLC24A5/SMARCA2/SYNE2/TYRP1): rsID NOT found in S6B — the S6B SMARCA2-region credible set (125 rows) contains a different, expanded SNP set (fine-mapping candidates) that does not include this main-text lead SNP by rsID. Honest gap.
source_location: Main text p.1348 (Novel Variants Influence Skin Pigmentation in KhoeSan Populations); Figure 5C

coord_build: GRCh37/hg19 (exact bp not given in main text)
beta_units: M index scale, targeted resequencing association, additive model
evidence_quote: rs7866411 (p = 8.91e-05) and rs2093835 (p = 1.17e-04) being most significantly associated with skin pigmentation
classification_notes: Same evidentiary basis as rs7866411 row above. Not found in S6B by rsID (honest gap).
source_location: Main text p.1348 (Novel Variants Influence Skin Pigmentation in KhoeSan Populations); Figure 5C

coord_build: GRCh37/hg19 (exact bp not given in main text; not found in S6A/S6B — honest gap)
gws_threshold: p < 5e-8 (paper explicitly labels this 'suggestive novel association', not genome-wide significant)
beta_units: M index scale, per derived allele, additive model, phase1+phase2 meta-analysis
evidence_quote: We identified an additional suggestive novel association in and near SNX13, with common derived T alleles of rs2110015 associated with light skin (b = -3.1, p = 1.3e-07 ... SNX13 regulates lysosomal degradation and G protein signaling but has not previously been associated with skin pigmentation
classification_notes: SNX13 explicitly NOT on the canonical effector list and the paper explicitly states it 'has not previously been associated with skin pigmentation' — a stated-novel, non-canonical candidate. However attribution_basis is nearest_gene_only: no eQTL/reporter/CRISPR evidence on SNX13 itself is presented, only genomic proximity of the lead SNP ('in and near SNX13'). Common derived allele (paper says 'common derived T alleles') distinguishes this from the other <5%-frequency suggestive hits. FLAGSHIP non-canonical candidate for this paper given its explicit suggestive-but-largest-effect-size framing.
source_location: Main text p.1349 (end of Novel Variants section); Figure 5D

coord_build: GRCh37/hg19 (FREM1 region chr9:14,724,664-14,910,993 per Table S7 targeted-resequencing capture design; EPM2A region chr6:145,550,394-145,806,673 per Table S7)
p_joint_str: not_reported (only stated as 'above 95% CI' on resequencing QQ plot, Figure 5A)
gws_threshold: p < 5e-8 (not applicable/not met — flagged only as a QQ-plot outlier above the 95% CI envelope)
evidence_quote: Multiple low-frequency (<5%) SNPs near several additional genes—including EPM2A, FREM1, SMARCA2/VLDLR, and TYRP1—are above the 95% confidence interval of expected versus observed significance (Figure 5). Two of these loci are near EPM2A and FREM1, neither of which have any known role in skin pigmentation in humans or model organisms.
classification_notes: The paper's OWN text explicitly states EPM2A and FREM1 have NO known pigmentation role — the most honest possible 'suggestive, unexplained' framing in this paper. No individual lead rsID or p-value given for these two loci in the main text (only a QQ-plot visual flag); S6B contains a large annotated FREM1-region SNP set (11 rows within the SMARCA2/TYRP1-labeled region tables, chr9:14.7-14.9Mb) but the paper does not name a specific lead SNP for EPM2A or FREM1 — HONEST GAP: exact lead SNP/p-value for EPM2A and FREM1 individually not resolvable from the two source PDFs as provided.
